items.q: Why do TNCs locate production in LICs?
items.correct: Lower labour costs, weaker regulation, access to raw materials, large potential markets, and government incentives like tax breaks in special economic zones.
items.misconception: Because TNCs want to develop poorer countries and lift them out of poverty.
items.random_wrong: Because LICs have better infrastructure than HICs.
items.why: Students attribute moral motives. TNCs are profit-driven; any development benefit is a side effect, not a goal — a critical distinction for evaluation questions.

items.q: How do headlands and bays form on a discordant coastline?
items.correct: Less resistant rock (e.g. clay) is eroded faster, forming bays; more resistant rock (e.g. chalk) is eroded slower, left protruding as headlands — perpendicular bands of geology.
items.misconception: Headlands form where the sea bends round to attack the land.
items.random_wrong: Headlands and bays form because of differences in sea temperature.
items.why: The pattern is set by geology (differential erosion of rock bands meeting the coast at right angles), not by water behaviour. Concordant coasts (parallel bands) produce coves, not bays.

items.q: Why is a food web a better model than a food chain?
items.correct: A food chain shows one linear path; a food web shows the many interconnected feeding relationships in reality, so it better represents how species depend on multiple food sources.
items.misconception: A food web is the same as a food chain, just drawn differently.
items.random_wrong: A food web shows what spiders eat and a food chain shows what other animals eat.
items.why: Most species have multiple predators and prey. A linear chain is a simplification — the web captures redundancy and resilience. Removing one species rarely collapses a real ecosystem.

items.correct: Reliable access to enough safe and nutritious food
items.why: Correct answer: Reliable access to enough safe and nutritious food. “Locking food in a safe” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which region has the LARGEST number of people without electricity?
items.why: It's Sub-Saharan Africa. “Western Europe” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many BILLION people lack safely managed drinking water globally?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
